questions.question: In a two-level virtual memory, the memory access time for main memory, t_M = 10^(-6) sec, and the memory access time for the secondary memory, t_D = 10^(-3) sec. What must be the hit ratio, H, such that the access efficiency is within 90% of its maximum value?
questions.to_find: Minimum hit ratio H so that effective access time is within 90% of its maximum (i.e., best-case) value

understand.plain: A two-level virtual memory uses main memory (fast) and secondary storage (slow). When a page is found in main memory it is a 'hit'; otherwise it's a 'miss' and the slower secondary memory is accessed. The effective access time depends on the hit ratio H. We want H to be large enough that effective access time is no worse than 90% efficiency relative to the pure main-memory access time.

keywords.explain: Fraction of memory accesses that are satisfied by main memory (page found in RAM).
keywords.example: H = 0.9 means 90 out of 100 accesses are in main memory.

keywords.explain: Weighted average of main memory access time and secondary memory access time based on H.
Ratio of best-case (pure main memory) access time to effective access time. Maximum efficiency = 1 when H=1.

understand.visual_alt: A timeline showing two-level memory: main memory accessed at 10^-6 sec on a hit, secondary memory accessed at 10^-3 sec on a miss. EAT is a weighted blend.

given.aim: Find minimum H such that EAT is within 90% of maximum efficiency (i.e., EAT <= t_M / 0.9)

terms.connects: Best-case access time when page is always in RAM

terms.example: 1 millisecond per access — 1000x slower than RAM
terms.connects: Penalty paid on a page miss

terms.meaning: Probability that a referenced page is in main memory
terms.example: H = 0.99 means 99% of accesses hit RAM
terms.connects: Controls the mix of fast vs. slow accesses in the EAT formula

terms.meaning: EAT must be <= t_M / 0.9, i.e., no more than (10/9) * t_M
terms.connects: Sets the upper bound on EAT from which we solve for H

given.plan: Write EAT = H * t_M + (1-H) * t_D. Set efficiency = t_M / EAT >= 0.9, which gives EAT <= t_M / 0.9. Substitute and solve for H.

steps.title: Write the EAT formula
steps.note: On a hit (probability H) we pay t_M; on a miss (probability 1-H) we pay t_D.

steps.title: Set efficiency condition
steps.note: Maximum efficiency (=1) occurs when H=1, so we want EAT close to t_M.

steps.title: Solve for H
steps.note: H >= 0.999, so approximately 99.9% of pages must be in main memory for 90% efficiency.

solution.result: H >= 0.999 (the hit ratio must be at least 0.999)

questions.formula_note: Effective Access Time formula for two-level memory: EAT = H*t_M + (1-H)*t_D; efficiency = t_M/EAT.

questions.question:
Indicate all the false statements from the statements given below:
A. The amount of virtual memory available is limited by the availability of the secondary memory.
B. Any implementation of a critical section requires the use of an indivisible machine instruction, such as test-and-set.
C. The use of monitors ensures that no dead-locks will be caused.
D. The LRU page-replacement policy may cause thrashing for some type of programs.
E. The best fit techniques for physical memory management ensures that the free memory available will never be fragmented.
questions.to_find: All FALSE statements among A through E

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of several OS concepts — virtual memory sizing, critical section implementation, monitors and deadlocks, LRU thrashing, and memory fragmentation. You need to identify which statements are factually incorrect.

keywords.explain: A code segment where shared resources are accessed; must be executed by only one process at a time.
keywords.example: Updating a shared counter must be in a critical section.

keywords.explain: A high-level synchronization construct that encapsulates shared data and procedures, with automatic mutual exclusion.
keywords.example: Java synchronized methods use monitor semantics.

keywords.explain: Page replacement policy that evicts the page not used for the longest time.
keywords.example: If pages 1,2,3 accessed and frame=2: LRU evicts the least recently used among them.

keywords.explain: When a process spends more time paging than executing, due to insufficient frames.
keywords.example: A process needing 5 frames allocated only 2 will thrash.

understand.visual_alt: Five OS concept statements — virtual memory limits, critical sections, monitors, LRU, and best-fit fragmentation — evaluated for truth or falsehood.

given.aim: Identify all FALSE statements among A, B, C, D, E

terms.meaning: Virtual memory size is bounded by secondary storage (disk) capacity
terms.example: If disk is 1 TB, virtual address space cannot exceed that
terms.connects: TRUE — virtual memory requires backing store on secondary memory

terms.meaning: Critical sections REQUIRE a hardware atomic instruction like test-and-set
terms.example: Peterson's algorithm uses only loads and stores — no special hardware instruction
terms.connects: FALSE — software-only solutions (Peterson's, Dekker's) exist without atomic hardware instructions

terms.example: Two monitors each waiting for a resource held by the other can still deadlock
terms.connects: FALSE — monitors ensure mutual exclusion but do not prevent deadlock

terms.meaning: LRU can cause thrashing for certain programs
terms.example: A program with sequential access of n+1 pages with n frames: LRU thrashes
terms.connects: TRUE — LRU is susceptible to thrashing under certain access patterns

terms.meaning: Best-fit memory allocation never causes fragmentation
terms.example: Best-fit leaves very small leftover fragments that are too small to be useful — external fragmentation
terms.connects: FALSE — best-fit actually causes MORE external fragmentation than first-fit in many cases

given.plan: Evaluate each statement against established OS theory. A and D are true; B, C, and E are false.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement A
steps.apply: Virtual memory uses secondary storage as its backing store. The total virtual memory available cannot exceed the size of secondary storage. Statement A is TRUE.
steps.note: Virtual memory size = RAM + swap space on disk, so disk capacity limits it.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement B
steps.apply: Peterson's algorithm and Dekker's algorithm implement critical sections using only ordinary load/store instructions — no indivisible hardware instruction required. Statement B is FALSE.
steps.note: Hardware atomic instructions (test-and-set, compare-and-swap) are one approach, but not the only one.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement C
steps.apply: Monitors guarantee mutual exclusion for their internal procedures but do NOT prevent deadlock. For example, if two monitors wait for each other's resources, a deadlock cycle can form. Statement C is FALSE.
steps.note: Monitors address mutual exclusion, not the circular-wait condition of deadlock.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement D
steps.apply: LRU page replacement can cause thrashing when the working set is larger than available frames. For a program with a cyclic access pattern of n+1 unique pages with only n frames, LRU evicts a page that will be used immediately next — leading to continuous page faults (thrashing). Statement D is TRUE.
steps.note: LRU with Belady's anomaly pattern or large cyclic access causes thrashing.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement E
steps.apply: Best-fit allocates the smallest hole that fits a request, which tends to leave many tiny leftover fragments that are too small to use — causing significant external fragmentation. Statement E is FALSE.
steps.note: Best-fit actually tends to produce more external fragmentation than first-fit; compaction is needed periodically.

questions.formula_note: OS fundamentals: virtual memory backing store limits, critical section algorithms (Peterson's/Dekker's), monitor mutual-exclusion properties, LRU thrashing, and best-fit external fragmentation.

questions.question:
Which one of the following statements is true?
A. Macro definitions cannot appear within other macro definitions in assembly language programs
B. Overlaying is used to run a program which is longer than the address space of a computer
C. Virtual memory can be used to accommodate a program which is longer than the physical memory of a computer
D. It is not possible to write interrupt service routines in a high level language
questions.answer: C. Virtual memory can be used to accommodate a program which is longer than the physical memory of a computer
questions.to_find: Which statement among A, B, C, D is TRUE

understand.plain: This question tests whether you can correctly identify truths about macro definitions in assembly, overlaying, virtual memory, and interrupt service routines. You must distinguish fact from fiction for each of these OS/assembly concepts.

keywords.explain: A memory management technique that gives processes an illusion of more memory than physically available, using disk as an extension.
keywords.example: A 4 GB RAM machine can run a 6 GB program using virtual memory with disk paging.

keywords.explain: A technique where different parts of a program share the same physical memory region — only one overlay is in RAM at a time.
keywords.example: A program with function A and function B uses overlays to fit both in limited RAM.

keywords.explain: Code that runs in response to a hardware or software interrupt to handle an event.
keywords.example: Keyboard ISR reads a keystroke when a key-press interrupt fires.

understand.visual_alt: Concept diagram: Virtual memory maps large logical address space to smaller physical RAM via page tables and disk swap.

given.aim: Identify the single TRUE statement among the four options

terms.meaning: Claims macros cannot be nested in assembly
terms.example: Many assemblers (like MASM) do support nested macro definitions
terms.connects: FALSE — nested macros are allowed in many assemblers

terms.meaning: Claims overlaying handles programs longer than the address space
terms.example: Overlaying works within the address space by swapping sections in/out; it cannot exceed address space size
terms.connects: FALSE — overlaying runs a program larger than physical memory, not larger than address space

terms.meaning: Claims virtual memory can run a program larger than physical memory
terms.example: A 2 GB program on a 512 MB RAM system can run via paging to disk
terms.connects: TRUE — the key purpose of virtual memory is to allow programs larger than physical memory

terms.meaning: Claims ISRs cannot be written in high-level languages
terms.example: Modern C compilers support ISR annotations (e.g., __attribute__((interrupt)) in GCC)
terms.connects: FALSE — ISRs can and are written in C with compiler support

given.plan: Analyze each option against standard OS and systems programming knowledge. Option C correctly states the purpose of virtual memory.

steps.title: Evaluate option A
steps.apply: Many assemblers (e.g., MASM, NASM) allow nested macro definitions. Option A is FALSE.
steps.note: Macro nesting is a well-known feature of assemblers.

steps.title: Evaluate option B
steps.apply: Overlaying allows a program that is larger than PHYSICAL MEMORY to run by dividing it into sections that share RAM. However, the program must still fit within the address space. Option B incorrectly says 'longer than the address space'. Option B is FALSE.
steps.note: Overlaying deals with physical memory limitation, not address space limitation.

steps.title: Evaluate option C
steps.apply: Virtual memory uses paging/swapping to allow a process's logical address space to be larger than physical RAM. Pages not currently needed are stored on disk and brought in on demand. This is the fundamental purpose of virtual memory. Option C is TRUE.
steps.note: Virtual memory extends apparent memory beyond physical RAM using secondary storage.

steps.title: Evaluate option D
steps.apply: ISRs can be written in high-level languages like C. Compilers provide compiler-specific keywords or pragmas (e.g., interrupt keyword in embedded C) to generate appropriate ISR code. Option D is FALSE.
steps.note: Even if some register-saving must be done in assembly, ISR bodies can be in C.

solution.result: Option C is TRUE: Virtual memory can be used to accommodate a program which is longer than the physical memory of a computer.

questions.formula_note: Virtual memory fundamentals: demand paging allows programs larger than physical RAM by using disk as secondary backing store.

questions.question:
In a paged segmented scheme of memory management, the segment table itself must have a page table because
A. The segment table is often too large to fit in one page
B. Each segment is spread over a number of pages
C. Segment tables point to page tables and not to the physical locations of the segments
D. The processor's description base register points to a page table
questions.answer: A. The segment table is often too large to fit in one page
questions.to_find: Why the segment table itself requires a page table in a paged-segmented memory scheme

understand.plain: In a paged-segmentation scheme, the logical address space is divided into segments, and each segment is further divided into pages. The segment table (which maps segment numbers to their page tables) can be very large. Since the memory system itself is paged, even the segment table must be stored across pages — and therefore must have its own page table to locate those pages.

keywords.explain: A memory management hybrid where logical address space is divided into segments, and each segment is further divided into fixed-size pages.
keywords.example: MULTICS used paged segmentation: segment table -> page table -> physical frame.

keywords.explain: A table that maps a segment number to the base address and length of that segment's page table.
keywords.example: Segment 2 -> page table at address 0x5000 with 10 entries.

keywords.explain: A table mapping virtual page numbers to physical frame numbers.

markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="#1D9E75"/></marker><marker id="arr3" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="#1D9E75"/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three-level translation: Segment Table (large, needs its own page table) -> Page Tables -> Physical Frames. The segment table's page table allows locating segment table pages in physical memory.

given.aim: Identify the correct reason why the segment table requires its own page table in a paged-segmented scheme

terms.meaning: The segment table can have many entries (one per segment) making it too large to fit in a single page
terms.example: A process with 1000 segments has a segment table with 1000 entries — easily spanning many pages
terms.connects: Because the segment table spans multiple pages, we need a page table to map those pages to physical frames

terms.meaning: Since main memory is organized in pages, the segment table itself must reside in pages
terms.example: Segment table entries at pages 5, 6, 7 must be tracked via a page table
terms.connects: A page table for the segment table tells the OS where each piece of the segment table is in physical memory

terms.meaning: To access a logical address: segment-table-page-table -> segment-table-frame -> segment's-page-table -> physical frame
terms.connects: This is the fundamental structure of paged segmentation

given.plan: The segment table is itself stored in paged memory. Because the segment table can be large (one entry per segment), it can span multiple pages. Therefore it needs its own page table to locate its pages in physical memory. This matches option A.

steps.title: Understand why a segment table needs a page table
steps.apply: In paged segmentation, all data structures — including the segment table itself — reside in paged memory. The segment table contains one entry per segment in the process. A large process may have hundreds or thousands of segments, making the segment table too large to fit in a single page.
steps.note: The segment table size grows with the number of segments, and can easily span many pages.

steps.title: Conclude which option is correct
steps.apply:
Since the segment table spans multiple pages, the OS needs a page table to locate each page of the segment table in physical memory. This is exactly what option A states: 'The segment table is often too large to fit in one page.'
Option B is about segment structure (not why the segment table itself needs a page table).
Option C describes the general paged-segmentation structure but is not the REASON the segment table needs a page table.
Option D is incorrect — the base register points to the segment table's page table, not a generic page table.
steps.note: The key insight: the segment table is itself a data structure stored in paged memory, so it needs its own page table when it grows large enough to span multiple pages.

solution.result: Option A: The segment table is often too large to fit in one page, so it requires a page table to map its pages to physical frames.

questions.formula_note: Paged segmentation address translation: the segment table itself is stored in paged memory; when too large for one page, a page table is needed to locate the segment table's pages.

questions.question:
In a virtual memory system the address space specified by the address lines of the CPU must be _____ than the physical memory size and _____ than the secondary storage size.
A. smaller, smaller
B. smaller, larger
C. larger, smaller
D. larger, larger
questions.to_find: Correct relationship: CPU address space vs physical memory size, and vs secondary storage size

understand.plain: Virtual memory allows a process to use an address space larger than what is physically available in RAM. The CPU's address lines define how large a virtual address space can be. For virtual memory to be useful, the CPU address space must exceed physical RAM. At the same time, the secondary storage (disk) must be large enough to back all the virtual pages, so the virtual address space must be smaller than secondary storage.

keywords.explain: The range of virtual addresses a CPU can generate, determined by the number of address lines.
keywords.example: A 32-bit CPU has an address space of 2^32 = 4 GB.

keywords.explain: The actual RAM installed in the computer where pages currently in use are stored.
keywords.example: A computer with 8 address lines but 4 GB RAM has physical memory = 4 GB.

keywords.explain: Disk (HDD/SSD) used as the backing store for virtual memory pages not currently in RAM.
keywords.example: A 500 GB hard disk can serve as backing store for virtual pages.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="480" height="160" viewBox="0 0 480 160" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="60" width="80" height="80" rx="4" class="hi"/><text x="50" y="95" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Physical</text><text x="50" y="112" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Memory</text><text x="50" y="130" text-anchor="middle" class="tag mut">(small)</text><rect x="140" y="20" width="80" height="120" rx="4" style="fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:#7F77DD;stroke-width:3;"/><text x="180" y="65" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">CPU</text><text x="180" y="82" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Address</text><text x="180" y="99" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Space</text><text x="180" y="125" text-anchor="middle" class="tag mut">(medium)</text><rect x="280" y="10" width="180" height="140" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="370" y="65" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Secondary</text><text x="370" y="82" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Storage</text><text x="370" y="99" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">(Disk)</text><text x="370" y="125" text-anchor="middle" class="tag mut">(largest)</text><text x="240" y="15" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink"><</text><text x="100" y="75" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink"><</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Size comparison: Physical Memory < CPU Address Space < Secondary Storage. Virtual memory sits between physical RAM and disk in terms of size.

given.aim: Determine the correct size ordering between CPU address space, physical memory, and secondary storage

terms.term: CPU address space vs physical memory
terms.meaning: For virtual memory to be useful, the CPU must be able to address more than what is physically present in RAM
terms.example: 32-bit CPU: 4 GB address space; 512 MB physical RAM -> address space LARGER than physical memory
terms.connects: Fills the first blank: address space must be LARGER than physical memory

terms.term: CPU address space vs secondary storage
terms.meaning: The secondary storage must be large enough to hold all the virtual pages, so address space must be smaller than secondary storage
terms.example: 4 GB address space; 500 GB disk -> address space SMALLER than secondary storage
terms.connects: Fills the second blank: address space must be SMALLER than secondary storage

terms.meaning: Physical memory < Virtual (CPU) address space < Secondary storage
terms.example: 256 MB RAM < 4 GB virtual space < 100 GB disk
terms.connects: This ordering is the fundamental constraint for a functional virtual memory system

given.plan: Virtual memory requires: physical memory < CPU address space (so virtual memory has more space than RAM) AND CPU address space < secondary storage (so all virtual pages can be backed on disk). Answer is C: larger than physical, smaller than secondary.

steps.title: Address space vs physical memory
steps.formula_raw: CPU address space > Physical memory size
steps.apply: The very point of virtual memory is to give programs an illusion of more memory than physically available. Therefore, the virtual address space (defined by CPU address lines) must be LARGER than physical memory. If it were smaller or equal, there would be no benefit from virtual memory.
steps.note: A 32-bit CPU with 4 GB address space on a machine with 512 MB RAM demonstrates this: address space (4 GB) > physical memory (512 MB).

steps.title: Address space vs secondary storage
steps.formula_raw: CPU address space < Secondary storage size
steps.apply: The secondary storage (disk) serves as the backing store for all virtual pages. To store the entire virtual address space on disk when needed, the disk must be at least as large as the virtual address space. Therefore the CPU address space must be SMALLER than secondary storage.
steps.note: If the disk were smaller than the address space, you could not back all virtual pages, making the virtual memory system non-functional.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.apply: First blank (vs physical memory) = LARGER, Second blank (vs secondary storage) = SMALLER. This matches option C: larger, smaller.
steps.note: The ordering is: Physical memory < CPU address space < Secondary storage.

solution.result: Option C: larger (than physical memory), smaller (than secondary storage)

questions.formula_note: Virtual memory size hierarchy: Physical RAM < CPU virtual address space < Secondary storage (disk), ensuring the virtual address space can be backed entirely by disk while exceeding physical RAM.
